segments.s628-g89: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning 'near' or 'at the vicinity of' (به + نزدیک). Persian often omits the copula here, so the whole phrase names a location: 'near Shangal'. شنگل is a place-name following the prepositional phrase, and نگهبان هند is an appositive/epithet meaning 'guardian of Hind' (نگهبان + possessor هند), with the possessor placed after the noun as is normal in Persian.
segments.s628-g90: ز ... تا marks an extent or range: ز (poetic variant of از) = 'from' and تا = 'as far as/until'. دریای قنوج is a genitive construction 'the sea of Kannauj' (دریا + name قنوج), and مرز سند means 'the border of Sind' (مرز + possessor سند). Together they specify the territorial span 'from the sea of Kannauj to the border of Sind.'
segments.s628-g41: رهاند is a third-person singular verbal form (from رهانیدن) used here as a general/present statement 'delivers/saves'. The phrase خرد مرد را is the object phrase with را marking 'the man' as definite; از بلا is a prepositional phrase 'from calamity' modifying رهاند. Word order: Verb + (agent/theme) is common in poetry, so the verb leads the clause.
segments.s628-g42: مبادا is a negative optative particle meaning 'may it not be' / 'let it not happen' and introduces the following clause as a wish. کسی is the indefinite subject 'anyone/someone'; در بلا is a locative/condition 'in calamity'; مبتلا is a predicative adjective/participle 'afflicted/stricken'. The clause therefore reads as a wish that no one be afflicted.
segments.s628-g43: نخستین نشان خرد is a noun phrase 'the first sign of wisdom' (نشان governed by خرد). آن بود is the copular remainder 'that was' (بود = past copula). The demonstrative آن refers forward to the explanatory clause introduced by که.
segments.s628-g44: که introduces the explanatory clause 'that...'. از بد here means 'of/against evil' (afraid of bad things). همه‌ساله in poetic usage means 'all the year round' / continuously and modifies ترسان (ترسان بود = 'was fearful'). بود is the past copula making the whole a descriptive past clause.
segments.s628-g45: بداند here means 'he deemed/considered' (not just bare 'knows') and takes the following noun phrase as its object: تن خویش را ('his own body') with را marking the definite/focused object. The possessive is split as خوی + ش (ش = enclitic 'his'); در نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in secret/hidden', indicating how he regarded or kept his body.
segments.s628-g46: به here is instrumental ('with/by') so به چشم خرد = 'with the eye of wisdom' (چشم = 'eye'). جست is a past-tense verb meaning 'sought' (sense 'to seek' rather than 'to leap' in this context). راز جهان is the object 'the secret of the world' placed after the verb in the concise, poetic word order.
segments.s628-g47: This is a nominal predicate with خرد as subject and افسر شهریاران as predicate noun: افسر (literally 'helmet') takes شهریاران in a genitive relation ('helmet of the kings'); بود is the past copula 'was'. Poetic word order omits the connective (ezāfe) and places the predicate after the subject.
segments.s628-g48: همان is an emphatic demonstrative 'that very' / 'the same', here resuming reference to خرد; زیور is the predicate noun 'adornment/ornament' and نامداران ('the famed/illustrious ones') is the group for whom it is the ornament. بود again is the past copula.
segments.s628-g49: The clause places the verb بداند (3rd-person present 'he knows') before its objects in a poetic inversion: بد (evil) and نیک‌مرد خرد (the virtuous, wise man / literally 'good-man wisdom') follow. Here بداند governs the following nouns: it means 'knows/recognizes', and the noun cluster نیک‌مرد خرد is best read as a compound idea 'the virtuous, wise man' (poetic apposition).
segments.s628-g50: بکوشد is a 3rd-person present subjunctive/habitual form ('he strives') with the prefixed بـ marking ongoing action; به داد is a prepositional phrase ('for/to justice') where داد is 'justice'; و links the clauses; بپیچد is another 3rd-person present verb ('he turns') with ز (poetic form of از) meaning 'from/away from' and بد repeating 'evil'. Together: 'he strives for justice and turns away from evil.'
segments.s628-g51: تو is the emphatic 2nd-person subject 'you'; اندازه خود ('your measure/limit') is the direct object phrase with خود reinforcing possession; ندانی is the 2nd-person present negative 'you do not know'; همی is an archaic/poetic particle here giving a continuous or emphatic sense ('ever/always').
segments.s628-g52: روان را uses را as the definite/object marker ('the spirit / soul' as a marked object). The prepositional phrase به خون در ('into/in blood') locates the action; نشانی here functions as a deverbal or verbalised use of 'نشان' meaning 'you mark / you make (a sign)' in a poetic present with همی omitted in the clause (or carried over), so the whole clause says 'you mark / set the soul in blood.'
segments.s628-g53: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'); تاجدار زمانه is a nominal phrase 'crown-bearer/sovereign of the age' (تاجدار = crowned/sovereign, زمانه = the age/world); منم is the fused form meaning 'I am' used here for assertion. Word order is standard poetic: condition followed by the speaker's identification.
segments.s628-g54: به links the speaker to the roles/qualities that follow ('with/regarding'); خوبی و زشتی lists the two poles 'goodness and badness' (زشتی focusing on moral ugliness); بهانه is 'pretext/excuse' and منم repeats 'I am' for emphasis. The clause means 'I am the pretext/occasion for both good and ill.'
segments.s628-g55: تو 'you' is the subject; شاهی کنی is a verbal phrase 'you do kingship / you rule' where کنی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive of کردن used with the noun شاهی ('kingship') — a common Persian periphrasis meaning 'if you become king' in a protasis. کی is the interrogative 'when / how (then)'; بود here functions as an auxiliary/modal 'would be' (poetic), and راستی is the noun 'uprightness / truth'. The clause asks rhetorically when truth could exist under your rule.
segments.s628-g56: پدید آید is a light-verb combination meaning 'come into being / appear' (پدید = manifest, placed before the finite آید 'comes'); از هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction'; کاستی is the noun 'deficiency / lack' and is the condition preventing راستی from arising. The whole clause states that lack/shortcoming comes from every direction.
segments.s628-g1: وزیر خردمند is a noun phrase with noun first (وزیر) followed by its adjective (خردمند) — 'the wise vizier'. بر پای خاست is a fixed, somewhat archaic/persuasive phrasing: بر (on/to) + پای (foot) + خاست (past of خاستن) = 'rose to his feet' / 'stood up'. خاست is classical past tense.
segments.s628-g2: چنین گفت is a reporting formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' — here the lemma provided lacks the direct 'said' sense, see senses). What follows (کیخسرو داد و راست) is the content of his speech: a nominal phrase where کیخسرو is the name/king and داد و راست are two coordinated predicates/adjectives 'just and true'. و links the two qualities.
segments.s628-g3: جهان is the subject 'the world'. از بداندیش marks separation or freedom 'from the ill‑minded' (از = 'from/of' with a sense of removal), بی بیم is a circumstantial phrase 'without fear', and گشت (past/become) functions as a copular/intransitive verb 'became' — together: 'the world became free of ill intent and without fear.'
segments.s628-g4: وزین here is poetic/compressed (etymologically و + زین = 'and from these') introducing the next clause; مرزهای رنج و سختی is a genitive chain 'the borders/limits of suffering and hardship'. گذشت is the past 'passed' or 'ceased to trouble' — overall: 'and from these borders suffering and hardship passed away.'
segments.s628-g5: مگر is the exception marker 'except (for)'. نامور is an epithet meaning 'renowned' modifying the proper name شنگل. از هندوان gives origin 'from the Hindus/India' (از = 'from'). The whole phrase means 'except the famed Shengel of the Hindus.'
segments.s628-g6: که is the relative 'who/which'. از here means 'with respect to' or 'concerning' (از + noun phrase), so از داد پیچیده دارد روان literally 'concerning justice (داد) convoluted (پیچیده) he has (دارد) a spirit (روان)'. دارَد is a present/past-habitual possession 'has'. The clause describes him as having a tangled/complicated sense of justice and a spirited or restless nature.
segments.s628-g7: ز is a poetic form of از 'from', followed by هندوستان 'India'. تا marks the extent 'as far as / up to', and در مرز چین 'at the border of China' gives the far limit; together they set the geographic range 'from India to the Chinese frontier.'
segments.s628-g8: ز here again introduces the source/agentive phrase 'from/of' and attaches to دزدان پرآشوب 'tumultuous bandits' (دزدان + postpositive adjective پرآشوب). دارد زمین is a poetic inversion where دارد (has) governs زمین (the land/country) — meaning 'the land has (is full of) tumultuous bandits.'
segments.s628-g25: بگفتند is a classical past/perfect third‑person plural (‘they said’). هرگونه از بیش و کم is an adverbial phrase meaning “of every kind / in every way, more and less” where از governs the pair بیش و کم; the order (هرگونه + از + بیش و کم) emphasizes variety/intensity.
segments.s628-g26: ببردند is a past third‑person plural (‘they took’). The following nouns (قرطاس و مشک و قلم) are a simple coordinated list of objects taken; word order is verb then direct objects, typical in older Persian narrative.
segments.s628-g27: یکی here is an indefinite subject ‘someone / a certain one’. نامه بنوشت uses the archaic/past verbal form بنوشت (classical past ‘wrote’) with object نامه preceding the verb. پر پند و رای is a postnominal adjectival phrase meaning ‘full of advice and counsel’ (پر = ‘full (of)’ modifying the following nouns).
segments.s628-g28: پر از دانش و آفرین repeats the ‘full of’ construction with explicit از before دانش (‘full of knowledge’). آفرین here functions as a noun ‘praise / acclamation’ governed by خدای (praise of God), so the phrase means ‘full of learning and praise (of) God’.
segments.s628-g29: سر نامه کرد is a causative/constructive phrase ‘made the head/heading of the letter’ (سر = ‘head/top’, نامه = ‘letter’, کرد = ‘made/did’). از نخست آفرین indicates that the word آفرین was placed at the very beginning (‘from the first “Afarin”’).
segments.s628-g30: ز یزدان is the poetic prepositional phrase ‘from (or by) God’. برآنکس is ‘upon that person’ and is followed by a relative clause که جست آفرین (‘who sought praise’), where جست is the classical past/preterite ‘sought’ and آفرین is the object ‘praise’. The whole clause reads as a benediction/statement about God’s praise upon one who sought it.
segments.s628-g31: خداوند هست و خداوند نیست is a paradoxical juxtaposition using the simple copula هَست/نیست (‘is’ / ‘is not’) in present tense to state both existence and non‑existence of God; the repetition of خداوند emphasizes the paradoxical claim.
segments.s628-g32: همه چیز جفتست states ‘everything is paired’ (جفتست = ‘is paired’). The conjunction و links to ایزد یکیست where ایزد (‘the deity’ / divine principle) is predicated as one (یکیست = ‘is one’), giving a philosophical couplet: multiplicity in pairs, unity in the divine.
segments.s628-g57: Word order is subject (آیین شاهان) + copula (بود) + infinitive (تاختن). آیین شاهان is a genitive phrase 'the custom/practice of kings' (آیین = custom here). بود is the past copula 'was'. The following infinitive تاختن ('to charge/attack') states the prohibited action; in older Persian an infinitive can follow a copula to express general habitual action.
segments.s628-g58: چنین modifies the whole verbal idea 'to do/act thus'. با marks the beneficiary/target 'with' or 'toward'. بداندیشگان is a plural agent noun 'those of ill intent / malefactors'. ساختن here functions as a general verb 'to do, to act (toward someone)' — a poetic/archaic use of ساختن rather than strictly 'build'.
segments.s628-g59: نیای تو is the subject ('your ancestor'). The structure follows Persian SOV: subject + object marker phrase (ما را) + predicate noun (پرستنده) + copula (بود). ما را marks 'us' as the object of the worship; پرستنده is a predicate noun 'worshipper/one who reveres'. بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s628-g60: پدر is the subject 'father'. پیش شاهان is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the kings'; following it, ما attaches to شاهان as 'our kings' (شاهانِ ما). بنده is a predicative noun 'servant' or humble self‑designation; بود is the past copula 'was'. The full sense: 'My/our father was a servant before our kings.'
segments.s628-g61: کس از ما is a negative/indefinite phrase 'none/anyone of us'. The verb نبودند is the plural past 'were not' agreeing with a plural sense in poetry; همداستان is a predicate meaning 'of one mind / in agreement'. The clause asserts that there was no agreement among us.
segments.s628-g62: The subordinating/complementizer که introduces the reported content ('that...'). دیر آمدی is a past-tense statement 'you came/arrived late' (آمدی = 2nd person past). باژ هندوستان is an adverbial phrase 'with the cry “Hindustan”' (باژ = cry/noise, here describing how the arrival was announced or proclaimed).
segments.s628-g63: نگه کن is an imperative 'look/consider' (نگه = look/watch + کن imperative 'do'). کنون روز forms a temporal phrase 'now the day/time'. خاقان چین is a genitive noun phrase 'the Khaghan of China' (خاقان = khagan/ruler; چین = China). The whole segment is an exhortation to observe the present time concerning that ruler.
segments.s628-g64: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. از چین is the source phrase 'from China' (از = from; چین = China). بیامد is the simple past 'he came' (3rd person). به ایران‌زمین marks the destination 'into the land of Iran' (به = to/into; ایران‌زمین = the Iranian realm).
segments.s628-g65: به تاراج داد is a verb phrase meaning 'gave to pillage/let be plundered' where به is the preposition marking the direction/result ('to/for'), تاراج is a noun used as the goal ('pillage'), and داد is the past tense verb 'gave'. آنک آورده بود is a relative clause ('that which he had brought') with آنک = 'that/which', آورده = perfect participle 'brought', and بود as the past auxiliary marking pluperfect ('had brought').
segments.s628-g66: بپیچید here functions as a past/formal verb meaning 'turned' or 'recoiled' (i.e., the consequence turned back); زان = 'from that' (referring to the preceding evil), بد = 'evil/ill', and the clause که خود کرده بود is a relative clause 'which he himself had done' with که = 'which/that', خود = emphatic 'himself', کرده = perfective participle 'done', and بود as the auxiliary ('had done').
segments.s628-g67: چنین هم همی بینم is an emphatic, poetic present statement: چنین 'thus/so', هم 'also/too' adding emphasis, همی is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning 'still/continually', and بینم is 1st-person present 'I see'. آیین تو ('your custom/practice') is the noun phrase that the speaker is characterizing.
segments.s628-g68: همان introduces 'the same' or 'that very' quality; بخشش here means 'generosity/grant' (not strictly 'forgiveness'), و = 'and', فره is the Zoroastrian/epic term for 'farr' or divine glory, دین تو = 'your religion/faith' (dīn), so the phrase lists two qualities of the addressee's custom.
segments.s628-g69: مرا ساز جنگست is a verbless/copular construction where مرا (me/for me) with را marks the affected party and ساز ('sāz' here = disposition/means/readiness) + جنگست ('is war') yields 'for me the fitting thing/disposition is war' or 'war is my calling'. و هم خواسته follows as 'and likewise (is) the desire' with خواسته as the substantive 'desire/wish'. Note the poetic economy: the copula 'است' appears fused in جنگست.
segments.s628-g70: همان لشکر یکدل آراسته describes the object of that desire: همان 'that same', لشکر 'army/host' (the noun being described), یکدل 'single‑hearted/like‑minded', and آراسته 'arrayed/adorned' as a predicate/adjectival qualifier. Together: 'that same army, united and arrayed.'
segments.s628-g71: ترا با دلیران من پای نیست is a negative existential statement: ترا (to/you, here topical/oblique for 'you') + با دلیران من 'with my brave ones' expresses the adversary; پای 'footing/standing' and نیست 'is not/does not exist' together mean 'you have no footing/standing (in the face of) my champions.' The word order is topical (ترا first) and نیست negates existence/possession.
segments.s628-g72: به هند اندرون لشکر آرای نیست is a parallel negative clause: به هند اندرون 'inside/inward India' sets the location, لشکر 'an army/host' is the thing expected, آرای (here poetic) indicates 'arrayed/mustered' (literally 'arrangements/ornaments'), and نیست negates existence — 'there is no army (for you) within India to array/muster.'
segments.s628-g9: به ایران همی دست یازد is a verbal phrase: به ایران (prepositional phrase, 'towards/into Iran') + همی (an older progressive/habitual marker) + دست یازد (دست یازیدن, an idiom 'to lay hand on / attack'). The copular/auxiliary is not explicit — the whole means 'he will/does lay hands on Iran'.
segments.s628-g10: بدین داستان کارسازی سزد: بدین (ب + دین here functions as 'by/with this') introduces the manner; داستان 'this plan/tale' is its object modifier; کارسازی is a noun meaning 'execution/doing of the work'; سزد is an impersonal present verb 'it befits / it is fitting'. Together: 'It is fitting to execute the work by this plan.'
segments.s628-g11: تو شاهی و شنگل نگهبان هند is a nominative/title phrase with an omitted copula: تو (you) + شاهی (kingship/king) + و (and) + شنگل (a proper name/title) + نگهبان هند (guardian of India). Persian often omits the verb 'to be' in such appositions.
segments.s628-g12: چرا باژ خواهد ز چین و ز سند is a question: چرا 'why' + باژ 'an alarm/cry' + خواهد (auxiliary/future 'will') + ز ... و ز ... 'from China and from Sind'. The structure asks why an alarm/uproar should arise from those regions.
segments.s628-g13: براندیش و تدبیر آن بازجوی is an imperative/advisory cluster: براندیش (imperative 'consider/think over') + و 'and' + تدبیر آن ('the counsel/plan of that') + بازجوی (verb-like 'seek out' / 'investigate'). Together: 'Ponder it and seek its counsel.'
segments.s628-g14: نباید که ناخوبی آید بروی is an impersonal prohibition plus fear: نباید 'one must not / it is not proper that' + که 'that' + ناخوبی 'misfortune / badness' + آید 'come' + بروی (literally 'upon you' here: verbal phrase indicating direction/impact). The sense: 'It must not be that misfortune befalls you.'
segments.s628-g15: چو بشنید شاه آن پراندیشه شد is a temporal clause: چو 'when' + بشنید (past 'he heard') + شاه (the king, subject) + آن 'that' (referring to the news) + پراندیشه (adjective 'full of thought / worried') + شد 'became'. Word order follows classical Persian: temporal conjunction + verb + subject + complement + copula.
segments.s628-g16: جهان پیش او چون یکی بیشه شد is a simile describing his perception: جهان پیش او 'the world before him' + چون 'as/like' + یکی بیشه 'a single thicket/grove' + شد 'became'. The predicate describes how the king's world seemed to him after hearing the news.
segments.s628-g33: ز چیزی (literally “from/for anything”) is a poetic indefinite: ز = “from/for”, چیزی = “anything/something”. کجا here is a rhetorical interrogative (“how/whence/would”), placed before the verb phrase for emphasis. او دهد is a third‑person singular present/factual form (دهد) used here with a sense of potential/conditional “would give”; بنده را is the definite object (را marks the direct object) “the servant/servant‑figure.” Together this clause poses the question “From/for anything, how/why would he give the servant?”
segments.s628-g34: پرستنده و تاج دارنده را are two coordinated noun phrases (worshipper and crown‑holder) that function as additional definite objects; the final را marks definiteness/ direct objects. The conjunction و links the two recipients; the phrase completes the previous question by naming who would receive (be given) anything.
segments.s628-g35: فزون ... نیست is a comparative/existential negation: فزون = “greater/more/above”, followed by از + noun to mark the standard of comparison (از خرد = “than wisdom”), and نیست = “is not/there is not.” اندر جهان is the locative (“in the world”). The clause therefore states that nothing in the world is superior to (or more abundant than) wisdom.
segments.s628-g36: فروزنده is a present‑participle/adjectival form meaning “illuminating” or “that which lights/brightens”; it functions as a relative qualifier referring back to خرد (wisdom). کهتران و مهان are two coordinated groups (“the lesser/lowly and the eminent/guests”) joined by و; together they say whom that wisdom illumines. Grammatically, this is a participial modifier (frozande) applied to the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s628-g37: هرآنکس که is a universal relative: “whoever.” او شاد شد is a simple past construction (شد = “became”) with او as subject and شاد = “glad/pleased,” so “whom He made glad” or “who He pleased.” از خرد marks the cause or instrument (“by/with wisdom”). The whole segment means “whoever He (God) made glad through wisdom.”
segments.s628-g38: جهان را is the definite object (“the world,” marked by را). به کردار بد is a prepositional phrase indicating the thing to which the world might be entrusted (“to evil/bad conduct”). نسپرد is the past negative of سپردن (“did not entrust/hand over”). Syntactically the verb governs the object and the prepositional complement: “did not hand the world over to bad conduct.”
segments.s628-g39: پشیمان نشد is an inverted negative past: پشیمان = “regretful,” نشد = “did not become,” so “did not become regretful/was not disappointed.” The following relative clause هر که نیکی گزید (“whoever chose goodness”) supplies the subject; Persian often places the predicate before the relative clause for emphasis. گزید is the past of گزیدن (“chose”).
segments.s628-g40: This clause gives the reason: که = “for/that/because.” بد آب is a metaphorical expression (“bad/impure water” — figuratively a poor source), دانش = “knowledge,” and نیارد functions as a verbal idea here meaning “does not bring” or “does not yield” (contextually ‘does not produce increase’). مزید (“more/increase”) is the object of that negative: “bad water brings no further increase to knowledge.”
segments.s628-g17: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus'). کاین is a contraction of که + این introducing a subordinate clause ('that this...'). کار من is a noun + possessor ('my deed/affair'), and در نهان is a prepositional adverbial phrase ('in secret') placed after the noun to modify how the action will be done.
segments.s628-g18: بسازم and نگویم are first‑person subjunctive/optative forms here expressing speaker's intention ('I will make/do' and 'I will not say' / 'I will tell no one'). به کس در جهان is a prepositional phrase ('to/for anyone in the world') where کس takes the object role of the verb نگویم.
segments.s628-g19: به تنها is an emphatic adverbial 'by myself / alone' placed before the verb; ببینم is first‑person subjunctive/present used as 'I will see / I will show'. سپاه is the object 'army' and ورا is the enclitic/directional 'to him' (show the army to him). The object+enclitic order is typical in classical Persian.
segments.s628-g20: همان رِسم شاهی is a noun phrase 'the same royal/customary rite' (همان = 'the same/that', رسم = 'rite/custom', شاهی adjectival 'royal'); و connects to گاه 'time/occasion', and the final ورا ('to him') attaches back to indicate these things are likewise for/to him.
segments.s628-g21: شوم is first‑person subjunctive/potential 'I will become/appear' used here as 'I will go / present myself'; پیش او is a locative/prepositional phrase 'before him / in his presence' (پیش used as 'in the presence of'); چون فرستادگان means 'as an envoy(s)' (چون = 'as/like', فرستادگان = 'envoys').
segments.s628-g22: نگویم again is the first‑person negative form 'I will not tell/say'; به ایران is a prepositional phrase referring to Iran (here more naturally 'the people/realm of Iran'), and به آزادگان 'to/among the freeborn' narrows who will not be told. The two به‑phrases mark the scope/recipients of the verb نگویم.
segments.s628-g23: بشد here is an adjectival/copular past sense 'it became / was made' (passive or resultative); پاک qualifies دستور ('clean/clear'), so بشد پاک دستور او با دبیر means 'his instruction was made clear/clean by the scribe'. با is instrumental/agentive 'with/by' introducing دبیر as the agent who recorded or composed the order.
segments.s628-g24: جزو ... آنک ... ناگزیر is a concessive/exceptive relative construction: جزو here functions to set off an exception ('except'), هرکسی 'anyone / whoever', آنک is a classical relative 'that / who which', and بد ناگزیر describes that person as 'forced/compelled (ناگزیر) to do bad (بد)'. Together: 'except those persons whom necessity/compulsion drove to evil.'
segments.s628-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خط here means the written line/script (the inscription). از نسیم هوا is an ablative phrase 'from the breeze of the air' giving the cause, and گشت (past of گشتن) + خشک forms a verbal-predicative clause 'became dry' (verb before adjective predicate, a common poetic order).
segments.s628-g82: نوشتند is simple past, 3rd-person plural 'they wrote/inscribed'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بر وی is the prepositional phrase 'upon it' (وی = 'it' referring back to the writing/paper). پراگند is used as a verb-like participle 'scattered' and مشک is its object 'musk' — together 'they scattered musk upon it.' Note the compact, somewhat inverted poetic order (verb then the prepositional phrase).
segments.s628-g83: به here marks the beneficiary/target of the action ('to/for'). عنوانش is 'his title' (عنوان + enclitic -ش). بر نام بهرام کرد is a causative/making construction: بر + نام + بهرام with کرد ('made'/'set') — 'he made (it) the name Bahram' or 'he gave him the title Bahram'. The verb کرد appears at the end as is normal for past tense in Persian.
segments.s628-g84: که introduces a relative clause 'whose/which'. دادش here functions as a noun with the possessive suffix -ش ('his justice'), not as a transitive 'gave him' (so read 'his justice'). سر هر بدی is the object phrase 'the head/top of every evil' (سر used figuratively), and رام کرد is a causative past 'made tame/subdued' — 'whose justice subdued every evil.'
segments.s628-g85: که is a linking/relative particle 'that/which'. تاج کیان is a genitival phrase 'the crown of the kings' (noun + noun). یافت is the past of یافتن used here in the sense 'obtained/received'. از یزدگرد supplies the source 'from Yazdegerd' (preposition + proper name).
segments.s628-g86: به is the temporal preposition 'in/on'. خرداد ماه is the month name (Khordad) + 'month'. اندرون here means 'within' or 'in the interior of' and links the month to a specific day; روز ارد names the day (Ord/Ard). The whole phrase gives the exact calendrical time: 'in the month Khordad, on the day Ard.'
segments.s628-g87: سپهدار is a title/noun 'commander' or 'general'. مرز is its complement 'of the frontier/border' (noun-noun apposition). و is 'and'. نگهدار is another title 'guardian/keeper' and بوم is its object 'the homeland/land' — a pair of parallel titles in apposition.
segments.s628-g88: ستاننده is an active participle/noun 'taker/collector'. باژ in the poetic context denotes a levy/tribute here (the exact lexical sense is archaic; no exact sense in the provided list matches, so marked accordingly). سقلاب is a place/people name (Saqālab/Slavs-like group) and و روم adds 'and Rome' (i.e. Byzantium) as the second region from which tribute is taken. The sequence is noun + object(s) in apposition: 'collector of (tribute) from Sclav and Rome.'
segments.s628-g73: An introductory phrase describing the addressee's state of mind. تو = 'you' (subject). اندر گمانی is a fixed poetic phrase meaning 'in the supposition/thought' or 'trusting the belief that…'. ز نیروی خویش = 'by/from your strength'; خوی + ش is the possessive construction (خوی = root, ش = enclitic possessive 'his/her/its' used here as 'one's own'). The whole segment = 'you, in the belief of your own force...'.
segments.s628-g74: Describes the repeated action. همی is a habitual/continuous marker ('constantly/keeps on'). پیش دریا = 'toward/before the sea' (prepositional phrase). بری modifies جوی (word order is adjectival after the prepositional phrase): بری جوی خویش = 'your wild/turbulent stream' (بری = 'wild, untamed'; جوی = 'stream'; خویش = 'one's own' as above). The verb is implied (drive/press) by the context; overall: 'you keep driving your wild stream toward the sea.'
segments.s628-g75: Simple past verb phrase: فرستادم = 'I sent' (past tense). اینک is a parenthetical/time adverb meaning 'now' or 'behold' placed after the verb for emphasis. فرستاده‌ای here functions as an appositive noun 'a messenger' (literally 'one sent'); the verb preceding the noun reflects classical/poetic flexibility in word order.
segments.s628-g76: Appositional description of the messenger. سخن‌گوی can be read as 'speaker' or 'eloquent one' describing the envoy. با دانش = 'with learning' (prepositional phrase qualifying the envoy). آزاده‌ای is an adjectival/noun phrase 'a noble-hearted one' (آزاده + the indefinite/relational ای). Together they specify the kind of messenger sent.
segments.s628-g77: Two stacked conditional clauses introduced by اگر ('if'). The first clause: اگر باژ بفرست — اگر = 'if', باژ = 'a cry/trumpet-call' (battle-signal), بفرست is the imperative/hortative 'send!' (here read as a conditional action: 'if you send a cry'). The second اگر introduces the parallel condition for 'war' (جنگ را); را marks جنگ as a definite/focused object.
segments.s628-g78: A command clause continuing from the conditions. به بی‌دانشی uses به in an instrumental/causal sense 'by/through ignorance'. سخت کن is an imperative 'make (it) harsh/severe' (سخت = 'hard/severe', کن = imperative of کردن). تنگ را is the object marked by را: تنگ here conveys 'tight/pressed' (the sense is idiomatic — 'put them to the squeeze' or 'close in on them') and follows the imperative; overall: 'make (the fight) severe through ignorance and press the (ones) tight.'
segments.s628-g79: A blessing-formula using the optative particle باد. ز ما باد = 'may there be from us' (ز = 'from', ما = 'we/us', باد = optative 'may it be'). بر جان آنکس درود = 'a blessing/greeting upon the life of that person' (بر جان = 'upon the life/soul of', آنکس = 'that person', درود = 'greeting/blessing'). Word order is poetic but follows Persian prepositional phrases.
segments.s628-g80: Relative clause introduced by که = 'who/that'. داد و خرد = 'justice and wisdom' (داد = justice, خرد = wisdom) are the qualities listed. باشدش is an older/poetic form where باشد = 'may it be / is' and the enclitic ش = 'to him' (i.e., 'be to him'). تار و پود = 'warp and weft' (weaving terms) used metaphorically: 'whose very fabric is justice and wisdom.'

sentences.s628-s45: Near Shangal, the guardian of Hind, from the sea of Kannauj to the border of Sind.
sentences.s628-s21: Wisdom delivers a man from calamity; may no one be stricken by calamity.
sentences.s628-s22: The first sign of wisdom was this: that one is continually fearful of evil.
sentences.s628-s23: He kept his body hidden, and with wisdom's eye sought the secret of the world.
sentences.s628-s24: Wisdom was the helmet (crown) of kings; it was the very ornament of the famed.
sentences.s628-s25: He knows evil; the virtuous and wise man strives for justice and turns away from evil.
sentences.s628-s26: You do not know your own measure; you are ever marking the spirit in blood.
sentences.s628-s27: If I am the crown-bearer of the age, I am the excuse for both its good and its evil.
sentences.s628-s28: If you assume kingship, how could righteousness arise when there is lack on every side?
sentences.s628-s1: The wise vizier rose to his feet and said, "Kay Khosrow is just and true."
sentences.s628-s2: The world became free of evil‑mindedness and without fear; and from these borders, suffering and hardship passed away.
sentences.s628-s3: Except for the famed Shengel of the Hindus, who has a convoluted sense of justice and a restless spirit.
sentences.s628-s4: From India up to the border of China, the land is full of tumultuous bandits.
sentences.s628-s13: They said: of every sort, whether more or less, they took paper, musk and a pen.
sentences.s628-s14: Someone wrote a letter, full of advice and counsel, full of learning and praise of God.
sentences.s628-s15: He made the heading of the letter ‘Afarin’ at the outset; from God (be) upon him who sought praise.
sentences.s628-s16: God exists and God does not exist; all things are paired, and the Deity is one.
sentences.s628-s29: It was not the custom of kings to charge — to act thus toward ill‑intentioned men.
sentences.s628-s30: Your ancestor was our worshipper; my father, in the presence of our kings, was a humble servant.
sentences.s628-s31: None of us were of one mind that you had come late, shouting 'Hindustan'.
sentences.s628-s32: Behold now the day of the Khaghan of China, who came from China into the land of Iran.
sentences.s628-s33: He gave to be plundered what he had brought; that ill turned back upon him which he himself had done.
sentences.s628-s34: Thus I still see your custom: the same generosity and the farr (divine glory) of your religion.
sentences.s628-s35: War is my calling, and likewise my desire is that the same host be united and arrayed.
sentences.s628-s36: You have no standing against my brave men, nor have you an army within India to array.
sentences.s628-s5: He will lay hands on Iran with evil intent; by this plan it is fitting to carry out the deed.
sentences.s628-s6: You are king and Shangal, guardian of India; why should a cry come from China and from Sind?
sentences.s628-s7: Consider it and seek counsel; one must not let misfortune come upon you.
sentences.s628-s8: When the king heard that he became pensive; the world before him became like a single thicket.
sentences.s628-s17: Would He give anything to the worshipper or to the crown‑bearer?
sentences.s628-s18: There is nothing in the world greater than wisdom — a light that enlightens both the lowly and the eminent.
sentences.s628-s19: Whoever He made glad by wisdom did not hand the world over to evil conduct.
sentences.s628-s20: He who chose goodness did not become regretful, for bad (or impure) water brings no increase to knowledge.
sentences.s628-s9: Thus he said, 'I will do this deed in secret; I will tell no one in the world.'
sentences.s628-s10: I alone will show him the army, and (I will show) the same royal rites and their timing to him.
sentences.s628-s11: I will present myself before him as an envoy; I will tell no one in Iran, not even the freeborn.
sentences.s628-s12: His orders were clearly set down by the scribe, except for whatever concerned anyone who was compelled to do wrong.
sentences.s628-s41: When the writing had become dry from the airy breeze, they wrote and scattered musk upon it.
sentences.s628-s42: He bestowed upon him the title/name Bahram, whose justice made every evil tame.
sentences.s628-s43: He received the crown of the kings from Yazdegerd on the day Ard within the month Khordad.
sentences.s628-s44: Commander of the frontier and keeper of the land, collector of tribute from the Saqālab and Rome.
sentences.s628-s37: You, trusting in your own strength, constantly drive your wild stream toward the sea.
sentences.s628-s38: Now I have sent a messenger — an eloquent, learned, noble-hearted man.
sentences.s628-s39: If you sound the cry, if you raise the war, make it harsh by (their) ignorance and press them tightly.
sentences.s628-s40: May blessings from us be upon the life of the one whose very warp and weft are justice and wisdom.
